topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I found the laptop open on the kitchen table, the screen illuminating a manuscript. My breath hitched, a tight, shallow thing in my chest. It wasn't just any writing; it was detailed, intimate, a tapestry woven from my most private moments, my smallest anxieties, the way I laughed when I thought no one was looking. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach, making my hands tremble as I stared at the words detailing my deepest vulnerabilities. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of mild surprise, as if I’d accidentally left the oven on. The silence that followed was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I didn't speak; I just pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood against the tile sounding impossibly loud in the sudden vacuum of air.

[story 2]

I watched him from the doorway, the glow of the screen painting shadows across his face. Every sentence seemed to land like a tiny shard of glass against my composure. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my neck muscles. He was so absorbed, so engrossed in this narrative he was crafting, completely oblivious to the landscape of my internal turmoil. When he finally paused, turning to me with that easy, warm smile, I felt a strange, hollow emptiness spread through my core. My eyes, suddenly too bright, met his, and I managed a weak, tight nod. "That's… a lot," I managed, the words tasting dry and brittle on my tongue. I turned away quickly, walking toward the living room, needing distance, needing to put physical space between myself and the weight of what I’d just seen.

[story 3]

The realization settled over me with a sickening weight, a heavy cloak draped over my shoulders. I picked up the printed pages, the paper cool beneath my fingertips, and the ink seemed to mock me with its unflinching accuracy. My vision blurred slightly at the edges. I stood there, rooted to the spot, unable to move, every muscle in my body locked in a rigid tension. When he finally came over, his voice was soft, laced with an apologetic confusion. "What is it?" he asked, his brow furrowed. I looked down at the manuscript, then back up at him, and the moisture gathering behind my eyes threatened to spill over. I shook my head slowly, a barely perceptible movement, my gaze fixed on a distant point. I simply whispered, my voice barely audible, "I need some space right now."
